title: What is an LLM? Check your understanding

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the correct mental model for a Large Language Model (LLM)?
options: A database that retrieves facts stored during training
A stateless function that takes text in and returns text out
A reasoning engine that thinks step-by-step like a human
A search engine that queries the open web in real time
questions.explanation: An LLM is a stateless mathematical function. It has no persistent memory, doesn't query a database, and doesn't reason like humans: it predicts likely next tokens based on patterns compressed during training.

questions.question: You make two separate API calls to an LLM. In the second call you include no context from the first. What does the model know about the first call?
options: Everything: the model maintains a session between calls
Only the most recent assistant message
Nothing: each call starts from zero
It depends on which model provider you use
questions.explanation: LLMs are stateless. Every API call is completely independent. If you don't explicitly pass prior conversation history in your request, the model has no knowledge of it: there is no session, no memory, no magic.

questions.question: You send 1,000 tokens of input and the model generates 500 tokens of output. How many tokens are you billed for?
options: 1,000: only input tokens are billed
500: only output tokens are billed
1,500: both input and output tokens are billed
0: billing is per API call, not per token
questions.explanation: LLM providers bill for both input and output tokens. In this case: 1,000 + 500 = 1,500 tokens. This is why a 128K context window doesn't mean 128K tokens of free input: your budget is shared between everything you send and everything the model generates.

questions.question: You're building a feature that extracts structured data (names, dates, amounts) from documents. Which temperature setting is most appropriate?
options: High (~1.5): for creative variation in extractions
Medium (~0.7): a safe, balanced default
Low (~0.0): for deterministic, accurate output
Temperature has no effect on structured extraction
questions.explanation: For structured extraction, accuracy matters more than creativity. A low temperature (near 0) makes the model pick the most probable tokens, reducing hallucinations and random variation. Save higher temperatures for brainstorming and creative tasks.

questions.question: A team asks their LLM to calculate compound interest on a loan. What risk should they be most aware of?
options: The model may refuse due to financial regulations
The model may confidently return an incorrect result
The context window is too small for maths problems
The model's training data does not include mathematics
questions.explanation: LLMs are not calculators: they generate plausible-looking text, not verified arithmetic. They can confidently produce wrong numbers with no indication of error. For any calculation requiring accuracy, use code execution or a dedicated calculator, then optionally have the LLM explain the result.
